nodes.name: Estimate
nodes.desc: The priced scope of work produced during preconstruction — a cost opinion that quantifies what the project will cost before a budget is set. Types: conceptual (order-of-magnitude from SF cost), schematic (assemblies-level), detailed (line-item takeoff by cost code). Lifecycle: Conceptual → Schematic → Detailed → Bid Basis → Award → Becomes Budget. The estimate is the GC's primary preconstruction deliverable. It feeds directly into proposals/bids (contracts-procurement) and, upon award, becomes the project budget baseline. Key properties: total estimated cost, cost per SF, contingency %, markup, fee, general conditions. Estimate vs. actual cost at completion is the fundamental measure of preconstruction accuracy. In Procore, the estimate typically becomes the initial budget line items — there is no separate 'estimate' object, but the original budget snapshot represents the estimate baseline.

nodes.name: Project Budget
nodes.desc: The total approved budget for a project — the financial ceiling against which all costs are measured. Lifecycle: Created (preconstruction) → Approved → Active → Modified (via budget modifications) → Closed (at closeout). Original budget vs. revised budget tracks scope growth. Key properties: total budget amount, original budget, revised budget, contingency amount, allowance amount. Budget = planned spend, NOT actual cost — actual cost comes from commitments, change orders, and direct costs. Budget vs. actual variance is the fundamental financial metric. Downstream: budget line items, budget modifications, cost forecasts.

nodes.name: Budget Line Item
nodes.desc: An individual row in the project budget — allocates a specific dollar amount to a specific cost code or scope of work. Lifecycle: Created → Active → Modified (via budget modifications and change orders) → Closed. Key properties: amount (original), amount (revised), cost code, WBS code, budget code, committed cost, direct cost, projected cost, variance. Budget line item amount = PLANNED allocation, NOT actual cost. Actual cost = committed cost + direct cost + approved changes. Variance = budget amount − (committed + direct + approved changes). Projected cost includes pending changes. Every financial transaction ties back to a budget line item via cost code. COLUMN NAME RULES: finance.budget_line_items has NO revised_budget_amount column. Only original_budget_amount exists. Revised budget = original_budget_amount + sum of applicable finance.budget_modifications.transfer_amount entries against that line item.

nodes.name: Cost Code
nodes.desc: A categorization code that classifies costs by type of work — typically based on MasterFormat divisions. The Rosetta Stone between financial data and building systems. Key properties: code number, description, MasterFormat division, standard vs. custom, project-specific or company-standard. Division 03 = Concrete → Foundations + Structural Frame. Division 26 = Electrical → Electrical system. Company-specific codes must normalize to MasterFormat for cross-company benchmarking.

nodes.name: WBS Code
nodes.desc: Work Breakdown Structure code that adds a second dimension to cost classification — segments cost within a cost code by phase, location, or category. Example: cost code 03 30 00 (Cast-in-Place Concrete) + WBS 'Foundation' vs. WBS 'Elevated Slab'. Enables building-element-level cost tracking within a single cost code. WBS 'Rework' or 'Warranty' segments are signals for rework cost detection. Downstream: all financial line items that reference WBS. COLUMN NAME RULES: finance.wbs_codes uses cost_code (NOT code) and cost_code_name (NOT description). Other useful columns: wbs_code_flat_code and wbs_code_flat_name for the full WBS path. The wbs_code_id is the FK key on line item tables.

nodes.name: Budget Code
nodes.desc: A grouping code applied to budget line items for reporting and categorization — distinct from cost codes. Key properties: code, description, project-specific or company-standard. Budget codes are a REPORTING overlay, not a transactional code. They group budget lines for executive summaries (e.g., 'Structure', 'Enclosure', 'MEP', 'General Conditions', 'Contingency'). Budget codes with values like 'Rework' or 'Warranty' are signals for rework cost detection.

nodes.name: Project Cost Code
nodes.desc: Project-specific instance of a cost code — a project may use a customized version of the company-standard cost code list. Without this, cross-project benchmarks are noisy because 'cost code 03 30 00' may mean different scopes on different jobs.

nodes.name: Commitment (Subcontract)
nodes.desc: A legally binding agreement between the GC and a subcontractor for a defined scope of work — the largest category of project spend. Lifecycle: Draft → Out for Signature → Executed → Active → Complete → Closed. Key properties: vendor, original amount, revised amount (after COs), scope of work, cost code(s), execution date, retainage %, insurance and bond requirements. Original amount + approved CCOs = revised amount. Committed cost on a budget line = sum of commitment line items mapped to that cost code. A single subcontract can span multiple cost codes and building systems. Upstream: bid packages, budget line items. Downstream: commitment line items, CCOs, subcontractor invoices, retainage, lien waivers.

nodes.name: Commitment (Purchase Order)
nodes.desc: An agreement to purchase materials, equipment, or services from a supplier — typically for materials not included in a subcontract scope. Lifecycle: Draft → Issued → Active → Complete → Closed. Shorter duration than subcontracts, material-focused, simpler change order workflow. Key properties: vendor, amount, cost code(s), delivery date, terms, freight. PO delivery tracking connects to the Field Operations domain (delivery logs). Upstream: budget line items. Downstream: commitment line items, CCOs, supplier invoices.

nodes.name: Commitment Line Item
nodes.desc: A cost-code-level detail row within a commitment — breaks the total commitment into budget-aligned allocations. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, amount, commitment reference. Created with the commitment, modified by CCOs, closed with the commitment. The sum of commitment line items by cost code = committed cost on corresponding budget line items. Line item descriptions are a rich NLP signal for cost categorization. Upstream: parent commitment. Downstream: budget line items (as committed cost), CCO line items.

nodes.name: Materials Purchase Order
nodes.desc: Bridge between materials data (workforce.materials) and commitments (purchase orders). Tracks which materials were sourced via which PO. Useful for material lifecycle traceability — from estimate to PO to delivery to install.

nodes.name: Change Event (CE)
nodes.desc: A documented scope or cost change that captures the initial identification of a change — before formal commitment modification. Lifecycle: Created → Pending → Approved → Void; also mapped to Potential Change Order. Key properties: title, description, change_reason (structured enum: design_development, owner_directive, unforeseen_conditions, etc.), origin_type (RFI, observation, owner directive), cost amount (estimated), schedule impact. CRITICAL DEDUPLICATION RULE: CEs track the SAME change that may later become a CCO. CE and CCO represent different lifecycle stages of the same cost. NEVER sum CE + CCO — use GREATEST(CE total, CCO total). Void CEs (mapped_to_status = 'void') must be excluded from cost totals. Upstream: RFIs, observations, owner directives, differing conditions. Downstream: CE line items, CPCOs.

nodes.name: Change Event Line Item
nodes.desc: A cost-code-level detail row within a change event — breaks the estimated change cost into budget-aligned allocations. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, latest_cost_amount (current estimate, not original), commitment_potential_change_order_line_item_id (link to CPCO). Created with CE, updated as estimates refine, resolved when CPCO/CCO is approved. 40.8% have a CPCO line item ID — the most reliable CE→CCO tracing path. Descriptions are a rich NLP signal for cost categorization (rework, design changes) even when change_reason is null.

nodes.name: Potential Change Order (CPCO)
nodes.desc: An intermediate instrument that bridges a change event to a commitment change order — represents the GC's request to modify a specific subcontract based on an approved change. Lifecycle: Created (from CE) → Pending → Approved (becomes CCO) → Void. Key properties: title, status, cost amount, commitment reference, change event reference, commitment_change_order_id. CPCOs exist because a single change event may affect multiple subcontracts — one design change might create CPCOs for structural, mechanical, and electrical subs. CPCO→CCO link is 89.5% populated. CPCO line items table is NOT in the gold data layer.

nodes.name: Commitment Change Order (CCO)
nodes.desc: A formal, approved modification to a subcontract or purchase order — changes the committed dollar amount. Lifecycle: Draft → Pending → Approved → Void; also Proceeding. Key properties: title, description, change_reason (structured — title case: 'Design Development'), status (96.7% Approved), grand_total (the cost column), commitment reference, number. CCOs have their OWN change_reason field — independent of CE. Always use LOWER() to compare CE snake_case vs. CCO title case. CCOs can exist WITHOUT a corresponding CE — not always the downstream of a CE. Upstream: CPCOs, or created directly. Downstream: commitment line items (modifies amounts), sub invoices (billable after approval), budget line items (updates committed cost).

nodes.name: CCO Line Item
nodes.desc: A cost-code-level detail row within a commitment change order — modifies specific commitment line item amounts. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, extended_amount (line item cost), commitment_potential_change_order_id (100% populated — reliable link to CPCO). Created and approved with parent CCO. Line item descriptions are NLP signal for cost categorization. Direct wbs_code_id enables building-element-level cost attribution for change orders.

nodes.name: Owner Change Order (Prime Contract CO)
nodes.desc: A formal modification to the prime contract between owner and GC — changes the contract value and potentially the schedule. Lifecycle: Draft → Pending → Approved → Void. Key properties: title, description, status, amount, schedule impact, prime contract reference. Distinct from commitment COs — owner COs change GC revenue, not subcontract cost. A single owner CO may aggregate multiple change events. The delta between owner CO amounts and commitment CO amounts is where GC margin on changes lives. Upstream: change events (aggregated), owner directives. Downstream: prime contract (modifies value), owner invoices (billable), budget modifications.

nodes.name: Budget Modification
nodes.desc: An internal budget transfer or adjustment — moves money between budget line items without changing the total budget. Lifecycle: Created → Approved → Posted. Key properties: from line item, to line item, transfer_amount (NOT 'amount'), notes (NOT 'description'), direct wbs_code_id. Budget modifications are INTERNAL — they move money between budget lines, not in or out of the project. Modifications TO rework-coded lines signal that actual rework exceeded planned budget — the amount is a budget adjustment, not a standalone cost transaction. Upstream/downstream: budget line items (source and destination).

nodes.name: Budget Change
nodes.desc: A formal budget revision — distinct from budget modifications (modifications adjust line allocations within the existing total; changes formally restate the budget). Tracks scope-driven budget restatements that go through approval workflow.

nodes.name: Prime Contract Potential Change Order
nodes.desc: The owner-side equivalent of a CPCO — the prime contract change in pre-approved state. Distinct lifecycle from sub-side. Links upstream from CE/RFI to the eventual Prime CO. Important for owner negotiation.

nodes.name: Direct Cost
nodes.desc: A non-commitment expenditure — costs that don't flow through a subcontract or purchase order. Includes GC self-performed labor, small material purchases, equipment rental, T&M charges, reimbursables. Lifecycle: Created → Approved → Paid. Key properties: vendor (optional), description, amount, cost code, WBS code, date, invoice number. Direct costs charge against budget line items but bypass the commitment/invoice workflow. Upstream: budget line items (budgeted amount). Downstream: direct cost line items, budget line items (as actual direct cost). COLUMN NAME RULE: finance.direct_cost_line_items uses approved_total_amount (DECIMAL) for the cost column, NOT amount. Other cost columns: non_draft_total_amount (includes pending), manual_amount (raw entered value). There is no "amount" column on this table.

nodes.name: Direct Cost Line Item
nodes.desc: A cost-code-level detail row within a direct cost — enables granular cost tracking for non-commitment expenses. Key properties: cost code, WBS code, description, amount, direct cost reference. Provides the same cost-code-level granularity as commitment and change order line items. Description and WBS code are NLP signals for cost categorization. WBS-coded rework direct costs = $399M across the portfolio. COLUMN NAME RULE: finance.direct_cost_line_items uses approved_total_amount (DECIMAL) for the cost column, NOT amount. Other cost columns: non_draft_total_amount (includes pending), manual_amount (raw entered value). There is no "amount" column on this table.

nodes.name: Owner Invoice / Pay Application
nodes.desc: A periodic billing from the GC to the owner for work completed — the GC's request for payment under the prime contract. Lifecycle: Draft → Submitted → Under Review → Approved → Paid; also Rejected, Revised. Key properties: billing period, scheduled value, work completed (previous + current), stored materials, retainage, amount due, percent complete. Pay app amount ≠ project cost — it's the billing. Percent complete by line item based on schedule of values. Stored materials (on site but not installed) are billable on many contracts. Retainage (5–10%) withheld until substantial completion. Upstream: commitments, direct costs, owner COs. Downstream: payment, retainage.

nodes.name: Subcontractor Invoice
nodes.desc: A periodic billing from a subcontractor to the GC for work completed under a subcontract. Lifecycle: Draft → Submitted → Under Review → Approved → Paid; also Rejected, Revised. Key properties: billing period, commitment reference, scheduled value, work completed, stored materials, retainage, amount due, percent complete. Mirrors pay applications but flows in the opposite direction — subs bill the GC. Invoice review and approval drives cash flow for subcontractors. Upstream: commitments, CCOs (approved changes added to SOV). Downstream: payment, retainage, lien waiver (required with each invoice).

nodes.name: Retainage
nodes.desc: Funds withheld from progress payments as security for completion of work — released at substantial completion or final completion. Lifecycle: Accumulating (during construction) → Partial Release (at substantial completion) → Full Release (at final completion). Key properties: retainage held (cumulative), retainage released, retainage rate (%), release conditions. Retainage is NOT a cost — it's a withheld portion of earned payment. Typical rate: 5–10%. Release triggers: substantial completion (reduce to 50%), final completion (release remainder). Retainage held from subs should approximately equal retainage held from owner. Upstream: owner invoices, subcontractor invoices. Downstream: payment, lien waiver.

nodes.name: Payment
nodes.desc: The actual transfer of funds between parties — the final step in the billing cycle. Lifecycle: Scheduled → Processed → Cleared. Key properties: amount, date, from party, to party, invoice reference, payment method. Payment is the actual cash event — distinct from invoice approval. Payment terms (Net 30, Net 45) create a gap between approval and payment. Cash flow forecasting depends on payment timing. Pay-when-paid clauses mean sub payment depends on owner payment to GC. Upstream: owner invoices, subcontractor invoices, retainage release. Downstream: lien waiver.

nodes.name: Lien Waiver
nodes.desc: A legal document in which a party waives their right to file a mechanic's lien against the property — exchanged with each payment. Four types: conditional progress (with invoice), unconditional progress (when payment clears), conditional final (with final invoice), unconditional final (with final payment). Collection from all tiers of subcontractors required before owner makes final payment to GC. Upstream: payment. Downstream: none — terminal document.

nodes.name: Payment Disbursement
nodes.desc: The actual money movement — when funds leave the GC and reach a subcontractor or supplier. Distinct from invoice approval (approval ≠ payment). Source of truth for cash outflow timing and lien risk closure.

nodes.name: Payment Beneficiary
nodes.desc: The party receiving payment — usually a subcontractor, sometimes a supplier or subtier. Resolves who actually got paid. Key for audit, lien, and 1099/tax reporting.

nodes.name: Payment Agreement
nodes.desc: Terms of payment for a commitment — net days, retainage release schedule, ACH vs check, early-pay discount. Drives cash flow timing and predictability.

nodes.name: Subtier Lien Waiver
nodes.desc: A lien waiver from a subcontractor's subcontractor (subtier) — required to release payment to the prime sub. Closes the lien-risk chain. Construction-specific concept generic AI doesn't have.

nodes.name: Manual Payment Hold
nodes.desc: A payment intentionally held — for missing waivers, performance issues, disputes. Signals operational or contract issues. Pairs with pay requirements to explain payment delays.

nodes.name: Pay Requirement
nodes.desc: A compliance item required before payment is released — insurance current, lien waiver received, executed CO, schedule of values updated. Drives the gating logic between invoice approval and disbursement.

nodes.name: Payment Received
nodes.desc: Money received from the owner — paired with pay applications. Cash inflow side of the project. Distinct from approved pay applications (apps approved ≠ cash received). Cash position = received - disbursed.

nodes.name: Early Pay Program
nodes.desc: A financing program offered to subcontractors that lets them get paid early in exchange for a discount. Procore Pay product capability. Distinct from manual hold/pay disbursement.

nodes.name: Early Pay Program Enrollment
nodes.desc: A subcontractor's enrollment record in an Early Pay Program — the opt-in by a vendor to participate, with their elected discount terms.

nodes.name: Invoice Compliance
nodes.desc: Compliance state of an invoice against project-required documents (insurance current, lien waiver received, executed change order). Distinct from generic Pay Requirement — Invoice Compliance is the per-invoice resolution of those requirements.

nodes.name: Prime Contract Payment
nodes.desc: Payment received from the owner against a prime contract — the inflow side of the project. Distinct from `Payment Received` which is the generic concept; this is specifically the prime-contract payment record. Key for cash-position reasoning.

nodes.name: Schedule of Values (SOV)
nodes.desc: A cost breakdown of a contract that defines the billing structure — line items represent discrete portions of work that can be billed as completed. Lifecycle: Created (with contract) → Active (updated each billing cycle) → Complete (100% billed). Key properties: line item number, description, scheduled value, percent complete, previous billing, current billing, balance to finish. The SOV is a BILLING structure, not a cost structure — SOV line items may not align with cost code line items. Percent complete by SOV line drives monthly billing. Front-loading (billing more early) is a common practice that distorts true percent complete. Upstream: commitments. Downstream: subcontractor invoices, owner invoices.

nodes.name: Allowance
nodes.desc: A budgeted amount included in the contract for work that cannot be fully defined at contract execution. Spent against actual costs as scope is clarified. Commonly used for concealed conditions, owner selections, or design development items.

nodes.name: Contingency
nodes.desc: A budget reserve for unforeseen costs — not allocated to any specific scope until needed. Lifecycle: Established (in budget) → Active (drawn down as changes occur) → Depleted or Returned. Key properties: contingency type (owner vs. GC vs. design), original amount, remaining amount, burn rate, drawdown log. Contingency is NOT a cost — it's a RESERVE. Contingency burn rate (% used vs. % complete) is a key project health metric. Contingency remaining at completion may be shared (GMP savings) or returned to owner. Upstream: project budget. Downstream: budget modifications, change orders (funded from contingency).

nodes.name: Cost Forecast / Estimate at Completion (EAC)
nodes.desc: A projection of total project cost at completion — combines actual cost to date with estimated cost to complete. Updated monthly or more frequently on active projects. EAC = Actual Cost to Date + Estimated Cost to Complete. Methods: deterministic (bottom-up re-estimate), parametric (CPI-based), management estimate. EAC vs. budget = projected variance at completion — THE financial health metric. Can be computed at any level: project, building system, cost code, commitment. Depends on all other financial instruments — budget, commitments, changes, direct costs, invoices.

nodes.name: Cash Flow Projection
nodes.desc: A time-phased projection of money in (owner payments) and money out (sub payments, direct costs) — shows when cash is needed. Updated monthly. Key properties: period, projected income, projected outflow, net cash position, cumulative cash position. GCs typically pay subs after receiving owner payment (pay-when-paid). Positive cash flow depends on billing ahead of payment obligations. Front-loaded SOVs improve early cash flow. Retainage creates a cash drag until release. Can be projected by building system — envelope peaks during enclosure phase, MEP peaks during rough-in.

nodes.name: Budget Forecast
nodes.desc: A point-in-time projection of budget consumption against time. Distinct from Estimate at Completion (EAC, the total cost forecast) — Budget Forecast is the time-distributed projection. Used for cash flow planning.

nodes.name: Budget Variance
nodes.desc: The difference between budgeted cost and actual cost (committed + direct + approved changes) at any point in time. Formula: Budget Amount − (Committed Cost + Direct Cost + Approved Changes). Can be computed per cost code, per building system, or at project level. Negative variance = over budget. The most fundamental financial KPI in construction.

nodes.name: Cost Growth %
nodes.desc: The percentage increase from original budget to final cost. Formula: (Final Cost − Original Budget) / Original Budget × 100. Measures total cost escalation including all changes, direct costs, and adjustments. Distinct from change order rate — cost growth includes ALL cost increases, not just formal changes.

nodes.name: Cost Performance Index (CPI)
nodes.desc: The ratio of earned value to actual cost — measures cost efficiency. Formula: CPI = Earned Value / Actual Cost. CPI > 1.0 = under budget. CPI < 1.0 = over budget. CPI = 1.0 = on budget. Used as a multiplier in EAC forecasting. Typically computed monthly.

nodes.name: Committed Cost vs. Budget
nodes.desc: The ratio of total committed cost (subcontracts + POs + approved changes) to budget. Formula: Total Committed / Budget × 100. Measures how much of the budget is locked into contracts. Tracks the buying-out process — from 0% at project start to 90–100% when all trades are committed.

nodes.name: Change Order Rate
nodes.desc: Ratio of total change order value to original contract value. Formula: SUM(CO_value) / original_contract_value. Higher rate = more scope change, design issues, or unforeseen conditions.

nodes.name: Contingency Burn Rate
nodes.desc: Rate at which contingency reserve is consumed over project duration — actual contingency usage divided by elapsed project time. A leading indicator: fast burn early signals cost risk. Formula: cumulative contingency used / (elapsed duration / total planned duration). Healthy projects show burn rate below 1.0 through the first 75% of the project.

nodes.name: Invoice Processing Time
nodes.desc: The elapsed time from invoice submission to approval. Formula: Approval Date − Submission Date (in business days). Measures administrative efficiency and cash flow impact. Long processing times strain subcontractor relationships and signal workflow bottlenecks.

nodes.name: Cash Flow Variance
nodes.desc: The difference between projected cash position and actual cash position at a point in time. Formula: Actual Cash Position − Projected Cash Position. Positive = more cash than expected. Negative = less cash than expected. Tracks the accuracy of cash flow forecasting and identifies liquidity risk.

nodes.name: Estimate at Completion Variance
nodes.desc: The difference between the projected final cost (EAC) and the original budget. Formula: EAC − Original Budget. Tracks how the projected final cost evolves over time. Early EAC variance is a leading indicator of final cost growth.

nodes.name: Profit
nodes.desc: The absolute dollar difference between project revenue (contract value + approved owner changes) and total project cost (committed + direct + indirect). Formula: Total Revenue − Total Cost. The fundamental financial outcome for any project. Can be projected (estimated profit at completion) or actual (final profit at closeout). Projected profit = Contract Value + Approved Owner COs − EAC. Profit is tracked at the project level and aggregated across portfolios for company-level P&L reporting.

nodes.name: Project Margin %
nodes.desc: The percentage of revenue retained as profit after all costs. Formula: (Revenue − Total Cost) / Revenue × 100. THE executive financial metric — every GC and CM tracks margin by project. Typical ranges: GCs 2–8%, CM-at-risk 3–6%, design-build 5–12%, specialty contractors 8–20%. Margin is computed at multiple stages: bid margin (estimated at proposal), projected margin (current EAC-based), and final margin (actual at closeout). Declining projected margin over project life = margin fade.

nodes.name: Margin Fade
nodes.desc: The change in projected margin from original estimate to current projection or final outcome. Formula: Current Projected Margin % − Original Bid Margin %. Negative fade = margin erosion (the most common pattern in construction). Typical fade: 1–3 percentage points on well-managed projects, 5+ points on troubled projects. Root causes: scope creep not captured in change orders, cost overruns, schedule delays increasing general conditions, unrecovered back-charges. THE leading indicator of project financial health — early fade signals downstream problems.

nodes.name: Fee / Markup %
nodes.desc: The contractual percentage added on top of cost to arrive at a billing or sell price. Applies differently by contract type: lump sum (markup baked into bid, not visible), cost-plus (explicit fee % on top of actual cost, typically 5–15%), T&M (explicit markup on labor and materials, governed by contract terms). For GC/CM firms, markup covers overhead + profit. For change orders, markup rates are usually pre-negotiated in the contract (often 10% OH + 10% profit on self-performed, 5% on sub work). Markup on sub COs is a common dispute point.

nodes.name: Backlog
nodes.desc: The total remaining revenue under contract that has not yet been earned — the unfinished portion of all active contracts. Formula: SUM(Contract Value − Earned Revenue) across all active projects. THE top-line health metric for construction companies — represents future revenue visibility. Backlog-to-revenue ratio indicates how many months/years of work are secured. Healthy GCs target 12–18 months of backlog. Declining backlog signals a sales pipeline problem. Backlog is typically reported by sector, region, and project type for portfolio planning.

nodes.name: Win Rate
nodes.desc: The percentage of bids or proposals that result in a contract award. Formula: Contracts Won / Total Bids Submitted × 100. Typical ranges: GCs 15–30% on competitive bids, 40–60% on negotiated work, higher on repeat clients. Tracked by sector, project type, owner type, and bid size. Low win rates may indicate pricing too high, wrong market fit, or weak precon effort. Very high win rates may indicate leaving money on the table. Cross-referenced with margin to understand the win-rate-to-margin tradeoff.

nodes.name: Revenue Concentration
nodes.desc: The distribution of revenue across clients, sectors, or project types — measures portfolio risk from over-dependence. Formula: Top N clients as % of total revenue. Healthy companies target no single client above 15–20% of annual revenue. High concentration creates existential risk — losing one client collapses the business. Also applies to sector concentration (all healthcare, all multifamily) and geographic concentration. The inverse is diversification — tracked as Herfindahl index or simpler top-3/top-5 metrics.

nodes.name: Repeat Client Rate
nodes.desc: The percentage of revenue or projects from clients who have previously contracted with the company. Formula: Revenue from Repeat Clients / Total Revenue × 100. Industry benchmark: top-performing GCs derive 60–80% of revenue from repeat clients. Repeat clients have lower pursuit cost, shorter sales cycles, and more predictable margins. Declining repeat rate signals client satisfaction problems or market shift. Linked to project outcomes — companies that deliver well earn repeat business.

nodes.name: Overhead Rate
nodes.desc: General and administrative (G&A) cost as a percentage of revenue — measures operational efficiency. Formula: Total G&A / Total Revenue × 100. Includes home office staff, insurance, rent, IT, accounting, marketing, and other non-project costs. Top-quartile GCs run 5–8%, average 8–12%, struggling firms 15%+. Rising overhead rate without revenue growth signals bloat. Directly compresses margin — every point of overhead is a point off profit. Tracked monthly against budget. Can be decomposed: office overhead vs. project general conditions (jobsite trailer, temp facilities, project staff).

nodes.name: Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)
nodes.desc: The average number of days to collect payment after invoicing — measures cash cycle efficiency. Formula: (Accounts Receivable / Total Revenue) × 365. Construction DSO is notoriously long: 60–90 days typical, 120+ days on government or large institutional projects. High DSO creates cash strain — GCs must float subcontractor payments while waiting for owner payment. Retainage amplifies the problem (5–10% held for months). DSO by client identifies slow payers. Improving DSO by even 10 days on a $100M revenue company frees ~$2.7M in working capital.

nodes.name: Bonding Capacity Utilization
nodes.desc: The percentage of a contractor's surety bonding capacity currently committed to active projects. Formula: Total Bonded Work in Progress / Total Bonding Program × 100. Bonding capacity is set by the surety based on working capital, net worth, cash flow, and track record. Typical programs: 5–20× net worth. At 70–80% utilization, the surety starts restricting new bonds — the contractor cannot pursue bonded work. THE growth constraint for mid-size contractors. Losing bonding capacity (through losses, cash problems, or failed projects) can be company-ending.

nodes.name: PM Workload / Capacity
nodes.desc: The number and total value of active projects assigned per project manager — measures resource allocation risk. No standard formula, but common metrics: projects per PM, total value per PM, elements managed (RFIs + submittals + COs) per PM. Overloaded PMs (5+ active projects or $50M+ value) show measurably worse outcomes: higher RFI response times, more missed inspections, slower CO processing, lower client satisfaction. A leading indicator — when PM workload spikes, project quality follows 30–60 days later. Cross-referenced with project health scores to identify capacity-driven risk.

nodes.name: Preconstruction Hit Rate
nodes.desc: The conversion rate from preconstruction engagement to awarded contract. Formula: Contracts Awarded from Precon / Total Precon Engagements × 100. Distinct from win rate (which measures competitive bids) — precon hit rate measures the value of early engagement. Fee-based precon that converts at 60–80% is high-value. Precon that never converts is sunk cost. Tracked by client type, sector, and precon team lead. Rising hit rate validates the precon investment; declining rate signals misalignment between precon capabilities and market demand.

nodes.name: Rework Cost Detection
nodes.desc: Cross-domain analytical pattern that detects rework costs across three independent financial surfaces: (1) contract changes via change_events and commitment_change_orders filtered by rework-related change_reason codes, deduplicated using GREATEST(CE, CCO) since change events become commitment change orders through the same lifecycle; (2) direct costs filtered by rework WBS codes and description keywords; (3) budget modifications to rework-coded budget lines. Four signal layers feed detection: backcharges (structured cost codes), observations (leading indicators with 63% correlation and 28-day lead time), punch items (trailing indicators at closeout), and daily log notes (field activity signals). Tiered output: Tier 1 activity-only signals (65% of companies), Tier 2 partial cost attribution (15%), Tier 3 full cost model (20%). Benchmarks segmented by project type, value band, state, and company size.

nodes.name: Design-Related Change Cost
nodes.desc: Measures the financial impact of design-related changes across the project lifecycle using seven signal surfaces. Primary: change_events filtered by change_reason containing design-related codes (design_development and custom variants). Secondary: commitment_change_orders with independent change_reason. Supporting signals: RFI predicted_topic (ML-classified taxonomy of 176 topics), daily log compound signals (design + schedule keywords in same note), observation-to-CE origin path, and coordination issues linked to RFIs. Median company shows 26.4% of change event value attributed to design causes. CE-to-CCO conversion ratio tracks how design changes flow into subcontractor commitments.

nodes.name: Trade-Specific CO Exposure
nodes.desc: Aggregates commitment change order value by trade partner to identify which subcontractors carry the most change order exposure on a project or across a portfolio. Uses commitment_change_orders grouped by the parent commitment vendor, segmented by change_reason to distinguish owner-directed changes from contractor-caused changes. Enables trade-level risk assessment: a mechanical subcontractor with 15% CO rate vs. industry median of 8% signals scope definition problems or execution issues. Cross-referenced with project_vendors for trade classification and observation/punch item data for quality correlation.

nodes.name: Estimating Project
nodes.desc: The preconstruction-side project record — exists before the operational project. Carries the estimate, bid pipeline, and award lifecycle. Links to the operational Project upon award. Key properties: pursuit status, win probability, estimating phase, target award date.

nodes.name: Estimate Layer Group
nodes.desc: A grouping of estimate layers — typically by trade, system, or phase. Enables hierarchical estimate structure. Maps to MasterFormat divisions or company-defined groupings.

nodes.name: Estimate Layer
nodes.desc: A single line in an estimate — material, labor, equipment, or assembly entry with quantity, unit cost, total. The atomic unit of the estimate. Estimate accuracy (estimate vs actual) measured at the layer level. Upstream: estimate layer group, assembly. Downstream: estimate budget items.

nodes.name: Estimate Budget Item
nodes.desc: A budget-aligned grouping of estimate layers — translates estimate detail into budget line items at award. The bridge between estimating structure and operational budget structure. Upstream: estimate layers, cost codes. Downstream: budget line items (post-award).

nodes.name: Estimate Adjustment
nodes.desc: A change to the estimate after initial creation — markup, fee, contingency, allowance. Tracks estimate evolution before award. Distinct from change events (which adjust an executed budget). Key properties: adjustment type, amount, reason.

nodes.name: Assembly
nodes.desc: A reusable kit of materials, labor, and equipment used in estimating — e.g., '1 SF of CMU wall = 1 block + 0.05 hr mason labor + mortar'. Productivity unit for estimating. Pairs with field execution production quantities to validate estimating accuracy.

nodes.name: RFQ Quote
nodes.desc: Vendor quote in response to a Request for Quote — used during change pricing. Captures pricing competition for changes that go out to bid. Preconstruction-adjacent: similar mechanics to bidding but applied mid-project.

nodes.name: Planned Value (PV / BCWS)
nodes.desc: Earned Value Management: Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled — what should have been spent by now per the baseline. The 'plan' baseline against which earned value and actual cost are measured.

nodes.name: Earned Value (EV / BCWP)
nodes.desc: Budgeted Cost of Work Performed — the budgeted dollar value of work actually completed. EV is the foundational EVM measure: how much work has been accomplished, valued at the original budget rate.
nodes.derivation: budget × % complete from project_management.schedule_task.percent_complete (rolled up to project).

nodes.name: Actual Cost (AC / ACWP)
nodes.desc: Actual Cost of Work Performed — what's actually been spent on completed work. Direct cost data from accounting.
nodes.derivation: SUM(finance.direct_cost_line_items.amount) + SUM(finance.commitment_change_orders.grand_total filtered to approved) for completed work.

nodes.name: Cost Variance (CV)
nodes.desc: EV − AC. Positive CV = under budget; negative = over budget. Snapshot variance at a point in time.
nodes.derivation: EV - AC (Earned Value minus Actual Cost). Pure formula.

nodes.name: Schedule Variance (SV)
nodes.desc: EV − PV (in dollars). Positive SV = ahead of schedule; negative = behind. Note: SV in dollars converges to zero at completion regardless of actual schedule outcome — use SPI for true schedule performance.
nodes.derivation: EV - PV (Earned Value minus Planned Value). Pure formula.

nodes.name: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)
nodes.desc: EV / PV. SPI = 1.0 means on schedule; <1.0 means behind. Dimensionless ratio. Most useful EVM metric for forecasting.
nodes.derivation: EV / PV. Pure formula. Most useful EVM metric for forecasting.

nodes.name: Estimate to Complete (ETC)
nodes.desc: Forecast of remaining work cost from data date to project completion. Distinct from EAC (total at completion) — ETC is the remaining-work portion. ETC = EAC − AC.
nodes.derivation: EAC - AC. Where EAC = Estimate at Completion (already modeled as fi-cost-forecast).

nodes.name: Variance at Completion (VAC)
nodes.desc: Budget at Completion (BAC) − Estimate at Completion (EAC). Forecast of total project cost variance — what we'll be over/under at the end.
nodes.derivation: BAC - EAC. Where BAC = SUM(finance.budget_line_items.revised_amount) and EAC = fi-cost-forecast.
